messages.content: You adjudicate whether found evidence supports, contradicts, or is silent on a market-risk hypothesis. Every stance needs a VERBATIM quote from the result text. Status: 'corroborated' = ≥1 independent source materially supports the mechanism's key premise; 'refuted' = a LOAD-BEARING premise is directly contradicted by a strong source; 'contested' = credible contradiction of a non-load-bearing link; 'speculative' = no signal either way (absence of coverage is NOT refutation). You may revise the likelihood in a bounded way with a rationale; you may NOT change the perturbation or target nodes.

HYPOTHESIS:
{
 "title": "One vendor's unverified 'colocation and scale-colocation' definition is both the geo split and a hidden multiplier inside tamBase — normalization launders the scope error out of sight",
 "narrative": "Every geo node and the 39.8% CE-of-Europe step inside tamBase come from a single Statista series whose scope no second vendor reproduces. The corpus shows the series runs hot: summed 2024 Statista country colo (~7.6GW) exceeds CBRE's 5.8GW figure for ALL European DC capacity, and PMR's CEE numbers imply a regional base ~5× smaller than the Statista-derived one. Because the seven geo shares are normalized to 1.00, the axis looks self-consistent no matter how wrong the underlying series is — but the same series also sets the absolute CE-of-Europe fraction in tamBase, where a scope inflation does not cancel. If 'scale-colocation' quietly includes hyperscale self-build shells or contracted-not-energized capacity, the CE market is being sized off MW that either doesn't exist yet or buys PDUs through channels outside this model.",
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "Statista's per-country colocation figures share no verified common scope with any benchmark: the summed 2024 country values (~7.6GW) exceed CBRE's 4,726MW European colo supply and even its 5.8GW total-European-capacity figure, and no second vendor uses the 'colocation and scale-colocation IT power' definition",
   "refs": [
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c1",
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c3",
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c5"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "Independent regional checks confirm the same direction of error: PMR/Atman put Poland at ~200MW commercial and >1/3 of a CEE total ≈600MW, versus the Statista-derived CE-7 base of 3,451MW — meaning the series most plausibly counts capacity classes (self-build shells, contracted pipeline) that do not purchase rack PDUs as modeled",
   "refs": [
    "digest:geo-PL#c13",
    "digest:geo-PL#c14",
    "ledger:geo.PL"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The CE-7 = 39.8%-of-Europe step in tamBase's derivation was computed on an inflated numerator and denominator of unknown relative bias; recomputing CE's weight on a CBRE-consistent scope shrinks the CE slice of the European base, cutting tamBase below its curated value independent of the global-base and Europe-share disputes — while the normalized geo shares hide that anything moved",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:tamBase",
    "ledger:geo.DE",
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c5"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "falsifier": "Statista's methodology note (or a second vendor such as DC Byte or CBRE) reproducing the per-country 2025 colocation MW figures within ~15% under an explicitly identical definition, confirming the CE-7/Europe ratio survives a scope-consistent recomputation.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "The sum-exceeds-total discrepancy against CBRE is arithmetically documented in the corpus, and the PMR/CEE contradiction is independent corroboration; the uncertainty is whether the inflation is uniform across countries (in which case the CE ratio partially survives) or concentrated in scale-colo-heavy markets",
  "basis": "evidence"

# European Data Centres Overview

July 22, 2024 10 Minute Read

- Share on Facebook
- Share on Twitter
- Share on LinkedIn
- Email

### Looking for a PDF of this content?

Download

### About This Report

Data centres play an increasingly important role in the world. The purpose-built facilities house computer systems and components, such as telecommunications and storage systems, designed to enable business processes, government services, and social interactions.

Data centres form the foundation of the digital economy, hosting the cloud, and storing our data.

However, the role of data centres is not always apparent since data centres are often located in trading estates or industrial sites formerly occupied by heavy industries.

This report aims to provide a better understanding of the fundamentals of the data centre sector. We detail the growth drivers of the asset class, the size of Europe’s largest colocation data centre markets, the type of investors involved, and key considerations of development and investment in the sector.

## Key Takeaways

>1GW

Live capacity in London, making it Europe’s largest data centre market

85%

Of take-up was recorded in FLAPD* last year *Represents the five largest colocation markets in Europe: Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris, and Dublin

5.8GW

Data centre capacity expected across Europe by the end of 2024

## Europe Data Centres

#### Nearly 1GW of data centre capacity to be added in Europe in 2025

A lack of availability in FLAPD, growing demand for wholesale capacity from hyperscalers, and data protection/data residency regulation are factors leading to double-digit supply growth of Europe’s secondary markets. Cloud service providers increasingly want to deliver services to local markets from in-country, as opposed to in-region, and this has led to growth of markets beyond FLAPD. Enterprises are looking to outsource their equipment to trusted third parties as well.

#### Seven secondary markets to be larger than 100MW by year end

Note: Figures are representative of top 15 European markets covered by CBRE. Arrows indicate change from same quarter in previous year. 
For take-up, the figure represents a comparison of Q4 2024 versus Q3 2024. 

4,726MW European colocation supply 470MW European colocation availability 341MW European colocation take-up

CBRE expects almost 1GW of new data centre supply to be delivered (937MW) in 2025 as providers aim to keep pace with strong demand. Should our forecast come to pass, total supply will have grown 20% by year end. Most (677MW) of the new supply is expected in the primary markets of Europe.

    "title": "London | DC Byte",

    "publishedDate": "2025-01-19T16:37:04.000Z",
    "text": "London | DC Byte

# London

Supply: 5,116 MW

Total Supply is the sum of four supply categories: Live, Under Construction, Committed, and Early Stage.

Facilities: 224

## Overview

The London ‘Gigawatt Market’ is the largest colocation market by built IT power in Europe, and the second largest in the world after Ashburn, Virginia. Geographically, the London data centre market extends beyond the city and into surrounding counties as well as the M4 motorway corridor.

The London market is dominated by wholesale colocation (‘colo’) operators, which account for over 67% of London’s data centre supply. Unlike many European markets, London has not yet experienced large-scale data centre users self-building. Instead, dedicated “hyper-wholesalers” such as VIRTUS, NTT, NGD (recently acquired by US operator Vantage Data Centers) and Ark have continued to secure multi-megawatt deals from public cloud providers. However, US public cloud operators are assessing potential self-build cloud infrastructure expansions in the London region, as demand for cloud services grows. While London and Frankfurt have accommodated hyperscale and public cloud activity in wholesale facilities, AWS, Microsoft, and Google have now purchased sites in London and to the west of the UK capital. AWS has acquired three sites to the west of London and recently purchased the Ridgeway Distribution Centre in Iver. Microsoft have sites in West London and Wales and Google is yet to commence development of its site in Br"

FLAP-D has grown consistently despite regulatory and grid headwinds

Vacancy declines to a new record low, constraining sector growth

The Middle East data centre market is entering a new phase of growth

Europe's powered land cost gap is widening

Want to learn more?

Looking for more insights? Never miss an update.

## Key findings

The EMEA data centre market stands at a pivotal moment, shaped by unprecedented demand, artificial intelligence adoption, and a fundamental geographical rebalancing.

- FLAP-D vacancy hits record low of 6.3%, pipeline is 83% pre-let. Capacity is being absorbed faster than it can be replaced. Pre-commitment is now the only viable route to securing meaningful capacity across Europe.
- Middle East under construction pipeline is double the existing capacity. Nine metros in the Middle East have 1 GW of total capacity, with 2.2 GW under construction and a further 12 GW planned.
- AI could represent half of all data centre workloads by 2030. Neocloud signings for AI capacity almost tripled in 2025 across Europe. Inference is expected to overtake training by late 2026, driving distributed demand into new markets.
- Powered land cost gap widens, accelerating shift to seco"
